speaker: interviewer
text: This is the interview with Dr. Helen Fagin. Today is February 11, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Fagin, how were you able to play the part of such a nice, little Catholic young lady that no one could suspect?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the easiest answer is, how could I not? Because if I didn't, I wouldn't have survived. And the trite saying, 'Necessity is the mother of invention'-- that's exactly what it was. While I was staying with that family, neighbors were looking askew at me. They weren't quite sure whether I looked Jewish, whether I may be Jewish, or whether it's just a suspicion of being different. And when my landlady told me, in a jest, laughing at it, she says, you know, [POLISH], somebody said that you may be Jewish. You look Jewish. And we laughed about it. Then I realized that I have to play it out to the hilt. Then I realized that my lifestyle has to be their lifestyle and vice versa, that I have to be beyond any suspicion whatsoever. And I became a very good Catholic. I learned the prayers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to mass. I knew when to kneel, when not to kneel. I knew how to cross myself. And I knew when and where to behave the way a Catholic does. And it simply was the necessary way for me to behave. As a matter of fact, she accepted me fully, to the extent that a friend of hers had a daughter who wasn't doing well in school, and she needed some tutoring. And she says, well, you told me-- I had told her that I have been educated in, in a-- in a good high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, well, you told me you studied Latin. I said, yes. She says, could you tutor Latin? I said, yes, I could, very well. And I tutored her friend's daughter in Latin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she recommended another to tutorial job. And very frankly, it sustained me quite well at this period to have earned a little extra money for the tutoring. As a matter of fact, one tutorial job paid me in goods, in food. So I would bring some to my younger sister, to the convent, and share some with my older sister. Anyway, we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we got through that period on, on, on, on the back of an awful lot of imagination, a lot of imagination. We had some episodes that were hair-raising. We knew that some people looked at us in a-- in a-- in a very suspicious way. We would have to leave from one trolley to another because we didn't like some looks directed at us. So we knew that we had to avoid, any way we could, being recognized and being informed upon. But the time was near when we realized that the Polish community is preparing for some kind of activity. Some people were talking about insurrection. Some people were talking about the Resistance. Some people were talking about mobilization. And I-- and then, all of a sudden, the rumors began to spread that the Russians are upon us in Warsaw, and that the Russian army is coming near Warsaw, and that they are disengaged from the agreement that Molotov and Ribbentrop had made in 1939. And they are at odds with themselves. And they are now enemies.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Russians are coming upon the Germans. And we better-- the Polish people, the Polish military decided that they better try to get into the act and perhaps start some kind of a resistance and perhaps retake Warsaw. That was in the summer of 1944, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my older sister and I got together. And we decided that we have to do something to get away. Now, in, in the meantime, while I was working at the [GERMAN], they treated their employees very well. And they had-- they took over a quasi-resort town, a, a sort of community. And they established some convalescent homes there. And they used-- actually, there were some convalescent homes there. They used these convalescent homes to send their employees, like, for a one- or two-weeks vacation, give them a little bit of a-- of, of a-- of a retreat. Well, because I was such a good, exemplary worker, they sent me away there. And I was trying to contemplate whether or not to accept it. And my sister advised me, yes, maybe it's going to be a good opportunity for us to investigate this place should we need an escape route. And sure enough, I went there. And I had made some very nice contacts there. And when I came back, I said to Wanda, you know what, in case we need to escape, this is a laid-back, little community. Nobody would be interested in us. This is the place we would go should the necessity arise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, at that time, when we realized that something was churning, and something was going to happen, I said to Wanda, you know, we'll go to Busko. We'll go to Busko. And we'll see what we can do there, whether we can go into hiding, or be on the outside. Busko would be a much better place than being here through the entire turmoil.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But what to do with [? Teresa? ?] She was in a convent. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I said to Wanda, for better or for worse, we have to-- [? Teresa-- ?] get [? Teresa ?] out of there because-- always remembering my mother's admonition-- we should try to be together and survive together. I went to the convent. And I spoke, spoke with Mother Superior. And I asked her if I could visit with [? Teresa. ?] I said, I was passing by, and I had a little something for her. And I took a bundle with me of civilian clothes because [? Teresa ?] wore a brown habit while she was in the convent. And when I saw [? Teresa, ?] I said, well, let's take a little walk. And while we were walking, I said, [? Teresa, ?] we are walking straight into the exit. And we are leaving. And she was a little nonplussed over it. And I explained to her that it's very dangerous to remain in Warsaw, [SIGHS] that we must leave, that we are not sure what we are going to do, but this is not the place for us to stay. And she said, OK, whatever you say.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came up to Wanda's place. And we-- sure enough, we got there. And the bombs started falling. And we had to run into the basement to hide from the bombs. And then we realized for sure that we have to leave Warsaw. Can you tell me what it was like while you were still working in the factory, the atmosphere of the Germans, with what was going on? Did you hear stories?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm glad you asked the question because I, I would have wanted to address it myself. First of all, one of the most disturbing-- most disturbing things for me while I was working there is to listen to the conversation of my coworkers, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] especially right after the ghetto-- the burning of the ghetto, how they were very pleased that, finally, they did the Jews in. And I had to just stand there and listen to it. I didn't have-- I couldn't go to the extent of chiming in to hope that they would not consider me different from them. But I couldn't bring myself to go in that far. As far as the Germans, we had nothing to do with the Germans. The Germans treated Poles as second-class citizens to begin with. So they wouldn't-- Germans would not lower themselves to discussing anything with the Polish employees. The Polish employees, however, did discuss the issues among themselves. And it wasn't pretty. It wasn't pretty at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they know, as far as you understood, about what was going on? Did they discuss the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no talk about camps. Nobody knew such a thing. Nobody, at that time, knew about camps. What we did know, after the Warsaw Ghetto, we did know that they took them away to the east. I did not hear the word 'Treblinka.' Although the transports from the Warsaw Ghetto went to Treblinka, I do not recall hearing the word 'Treblinka' until liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nor 'Auschwitz'? Nor--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not hear the word 'Auschwitz' until liberation. I did not. We knew that there were those horrible camps, labor camps. And, as I said, we knew that nobody returned from those labor camps. But I did not have any idea that there were gas chambers. Understand, we did not-- after we left Warsaw, we did not have any access to the underground. We did not know what was coming through the shortwave radio. As a matter of fact, I did not know-- after the very moment that I left the Warsaw ghetto, I did not know-- I did not get any information. Because, obviously, the German newspapers were only hailing the German victories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a Pole, I could read a German newspaper, the official German newspaper. But all you read there is about their victories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I, I really do not recall the knowledge of any of the gas chambers, or the ovens, or, or, or the death camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of conversations could be overheard by the Germans when you were in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: This wasn't a factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean-- I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in an office.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in the-- in the office.

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh. Well, I, I don't think that the Poles themselves would have discussed anything in the earshot-- within the earshot of German. I mean, the German were-- the, the Germans were their enemies. And they abhorred them greatly, with the exception of some of the people-- so-called 'Volksdeutsche.' Volksdeutsche were people-- Poles-- who were of German origin. And because they were of German origin, when the Germans came, they knew that they could, number one, serve the Germans; number two, receive very preferential treatment. So these Volksdeutsche were very, very helpful to the Germans in discovering those Poles who were anti-Nazi and, of course, Jews. But the conversation among the Poles was very private. And they abhorred the, the Germans. And they hated them just as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you and your sisters were planning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: --to travel together?

speaker: interviewee
text: --we, we went-- we, we, we decided to take the train. We-- what we later found out-- was the last train leaving Warsaw, when we got to Busko. While we came to Busko, I realized that it was an altogether different place from the time that I had left it. It was swarming with Germans. The front was very close to Busko. It was, like, 20 kilometers east of Busko. And they decided to establish a field hospital in one of those convalescent homes. And they had many nurses and many doctors there from Germany, the field nurses and field doctors. And the soldiers were being brought constantly into the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister got the brilliant idea that she's going to tell them that she has nursing skills. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went up there, in another case of real chutzpah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She says, well, I know how to take care of the sick. I know how to put up bandages. And I know how to do this and that and the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this is the first time in her life that she gave an injection. And she developed, while she was working there, she-- indeed, she was a very bright woman, young girl. She developed these skills. And she really proved herself to be quite proficient. And while she was able to get the job, she recommended me as her cousin for the office. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this was your older sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fela.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was fluent in German. And I was very good in writing German and doing German correspondence. And they did not have very many Polish people in that small community who could fit this bill. So they hired me for the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided, well, we have jobs. And we ate there. They gave us food there to eat. So we-- so we rented a small little cottage. And we put [? Teresa ?] into the cottage. And we brought her the food. And we kept her under the wraps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, finally, we decided, no. There was such a chaos there that nobody's going to be preoccupied whether or not she looks Jewish. So I was able to find out from somebody in the office whether they could employ a young girl who was an orphan doing anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, would she mind cleaning toilets? I said, no, she wouldn't mind cleaning, cleaning toilets. So I went back home. And I said, well, would you very much mind doing it? She said, no, I just want to get out. How old was she then?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was about-- probably about 14 years old. So she went to cleaning toilets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know how it came about that one of the commandants asked her if she would have some time-- not if she would have some time, but he would like her to go to his house and help his wife with the housecleaning. [LAUGHS] She could not say no. She came home. And she told us about the predicament. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, of course, we decided that we had no choice because if she said no, number one, it would be suspicious. Number two, she would only elicit anger from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she went. And she served as a maid in his house. They liked her so well that he wanted to send her back to Germany with his wife. And of course, she had great difficulty to refuse that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, what happened in Busko was something we did not foresee. It was the very place that the front was moving back and forth. And at one time, they evacuated the hospital because the front was coming very close. And the Germans were on this-- on their tail. And one time, we get up, and there were no Germans there. The hospital is evacuated. There is no sign of any Germans. And we go outside. And all we hear is distant noises of artillery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we heard the bombs falling. And the bombs started to fall. We didn't know whether these were Russian bombs or German bombs. So we had to hide again. We had to go into-- there was a big, big underground passage under that big convalescent house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we hid there. And the bombs were falling. And after the bombs stopped falling, we came out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Russians are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided to lie low, not to divulge our, our identity, just to play along the way the others played along and waited. And whatever the Poles said, however the Poles behaved, we just chimed in. And all they wanted-- they didn't want the Russians. They didn't want the Russians. They want the Poles to liberate them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, of course, the Russians were coming. And the Russians were communists. And the Poles did not like communists. So there was that dichotomy there between wanting to be liberated and not wanting to be under communism, under communist regime or risking the Germans coming back. Well, we were playing the role of Poles who were absolutely neutral. And we waited and waited. And sure enough, three or four days later, the Russians disappeared. And the Germans appeared. And the see-saw was going, back and forth, a number of times. And it was absolutely nerve-wrenching for us. It was so nerve-wracking that we just didn't know what was going to happen. We had no knowledge of how strong the Russians were. We had no knowledge of how weak the Germans were. But all we knew is that they suffered tremendous casualties because we saw those soldiers coming in near death, many dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we waited it out. We waited it out until the Russians returned for good. When the Russians returned for good, after about a week or so, we decided that we have to plan our emergence into liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the entire episode of liberation, it was a very curious-- emotionally, psychologically, curious period. There was no elation. There was no happiness. We knew we were liberated. And I felt, and I always repeated it-- we were liberated from sure death. That we knew, that no one is out there who is going to want to kill us because we are Jewish. Although, even in that, we were wrong. Because in 1946, in Poland, not from-- far from Busko, in a-- in a town named Kielce, K-I-E-L-C-E, where, in early 1946, people would be returning from concentration camps, from DP camps, and such, there was a pogrom where Poles killed returning, returning survivors of the camps. But we didn't know that that was any kind of a possibility. So at the moment of liberation, we felt that we were liberated from that angst, from the anxiety, from the anguish of whether or not we can survive yet another day. But we were not liberated from life. We didn't know what life has in store for us. We didn't know what to do. I mean, there was such a chaos. People were coming and going. People didn't know where to go, whom to search for. We wanted to know whether or not there was any chance to, to find our parents. We want to let people know, or whoever survived, that we have survived because different people survived under such different circumstances. Nobody knew the whereabouts of another person.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started traveling. We started traveling out of Busko, going in the direction of our hometown, Radomsko. And then we thought-- we will-- we knew we cannot stay in Radomsko. We knew our house was nonexistent. And we didn't know if anybody could make a life there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we got-- after a long, long, treacherous journey, by all modes of transportation-- by foot, by wagon, by-- partly by train-- we reached Radomsko. And, as I previously mentioned, we, of course, went to Mr. Loszek and Mrs. Loszek to thank them for actually saving our lives. We contacted Henryk. And we thanked him for saving our lives. We told the Loszeks that we are going to get all the papers-- my, my parents own real estate in Lodz and Radomsko-- that we are going to get all the real-estate papers in order and transfer all of the holdings to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went to that person whom our parents entrusted all the silver, and all the furs, who told us he had never received it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did give me a pillowcase. How would he have thought that I would not have known that something was contained in that pillowcase? And the pillowcase has my mother's monogram because this was her trousseau. And she had all her linens monogrammed in her monogram. And the pillowcase got so stained and yellowed-- it was pure linen-- that what I did, I cut just the monogram to be framed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was nobody in Radomsko. We couldn't find any Jews in Radomsko. But there was one occurrence that I would like to mention that happened there. I told you that I had a makeshift, clandestine school in Radomsko in the ghetto. And there were many girls there. I had about 20 girls there. And only four, I believe, survived, two of whom came to the United States. One of the girls, unbeknownst to me, was keeping a diary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was one of the very few people who were not liquidated with the liquidation of the ghetto. She was in hiding. And a few days after the auction, they-- not the 'auction,' the action, they sniffed her out. She was among a group of 11 people whom they took out of a bunker, and they marched to the cemetery. And they shot them. Now, before they did-- they, they shot her, she had her diary. And she thrusted that diary into the hands of a Polish policeman. And she says, before you kill me, you can take my life away, but please keep this. And the first Jew you see after the war who comes to the cemetery to visit Radomsko or the cemetery, please give it to them. And sure enough, after the war, another friend whose husband had died during the-- from typhus during the ghetto came to Radomsko to visit his grave. And an old woman approached her. This is the stuff that movies are made of. An old woman approached her. And she says, don't ask me any questions. My son got this from somebody. And they told me to give it to the first person I see who is Jewish. And she gave it to her. And that woman later on went to Israel. Now, I have the typed original-- somebody typed. I had the original in Polish. It had been translated into Hebrew. It had been translated into Yiddish. And somebody I know from our town bought-- wrote a book based on her diary. And-- while she also interviewed a number of survivors from Radomsko who lived in South Africa and in Israel. And I'm glad to see that I am mentioned several times in that diary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the book?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something I'll show it to you. It was given to me. It was published, I think, in South Africa or in England. I have it. I just showed it to my son. So he saw-- he saw the references to me. It says, 'Miss [NON-ENGLISH] N.' And then she describes Radomsko as the town. And she describes-- one of the markers for Radomsko was my father's store. And she spells our name there, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you learn of the fate of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we returned-- after we left Radomsko, we went to Warsaw. And we found some very dear friends of ours who-- and there is a side story to that. But I won't go into it. He is not Jewish. And she is Jewish. And he is one who is also Yad Vashem, the Righteous Gentile, the Avenue of the Righteous Gentile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please go into the side story?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very unusual side story. He was a son of a, a very wealthy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] aristocratic Polish family of German heritage. And when the Germans came to town-- and he was one of very, very few people that we knew who actually consorted with, with Jewish, Jewish friends. His best friend was, was, was a Jewish boy. And he was so very unusual. Well, he fell in love with this Jewish girl, who, who was a very, very pretty girl. And he saved her life. He saved much of her family's life. And he saved many, many, many, many other lives. And when we came to Warsaw, we found out that they lived in Warsaw. They took us in. I mean, they, they were just so gracious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had some very, very tight connections with the government. And he says, well, if you want a job with the new government, Polish government, which was patronized by the communists-- he was a leftist. And sure enough, I got a job in the-- in the Department of Education. And I stayed there for a short time until I found a way to go to the Displaced Persons camp.
